preamble: The voiceless retroflex sibilant [ʂ] — the value of Russian ш and of the ruki sibilant in the satem languages — backs to the voiceless velar fricative [x] through retraction and loss of sibilance. It is visible as a living dialect cline in Pashto and, in deep time, as the Indo-European ruki outcome that separates Indic (which keeps [ʂ]) from Slavic (which retracted it to [x]).

examples.note: The letter ښ is [ʂ] in the conservative Southwestern (Kandahar) dialect, [ç] in the Northwest, and [x] in the Northeastern dialects (Kabul, Peshawar, Jalalabad). The language name itself is [pəʂˈto] in Kandahar but [pəxˈto] in Kabul, a medieval fortition of the retroflex fricative used as the primary marker of Pashto dialect zones.

The ruki sibilant was retroflex-like before Slavic retracted it to velar *x, whereas Indic kept [ʂ] (Sanskrit ष). From PIE *wers- 'top': Sanskrit várṣman- [ʂ] 'height, summit' beside Proto-Slavic *vьrxъ → Russian верх [vʲerx].

sources: David, A. B. (2014). Descriptive Grammar of Pashto and its Dialects. De Gruyter Mouton.
Henderson, M. M. T. (1983). Four Varieties of Pashto. Journal of the American Oriental Society, 103(3), 595–597.
Collinge, N. E. (1985). The Laws of Indo-European. John Benjamins.
Shevelov, G. Y. (1964). A Prehistory of Slavic. Carl Winter.
